events.text: In 1973, Egypt and Syria launched a surprise two-front assault on Israel during the Yom Kippur War to reclaim territory lost in the Six-Day War. While no party made significant territorial gains, Egyptian President Anwar al-Sadat declared symbolic victory, facilitating future negotiations.
events.short_text: Egypt and Syria attacked Israel in 1973 Yom Kippur War; outcome was symbolic.

events.text: In 1979, following years of conflict and negotiation, Egypt and Israel signed the Camp David Accords, officially ending hostilities and normalizing diplomatic relations between the two countries.
events.short_text: Egypt and Israel signed peace treaty in 1979 via Camp David Accords.

events.text: Despite improved Israeli-Arab relations after 1979, unresolved issues around Palestinian self-governance led to the outbreak of the first intifada in 1987, during which hundreds of thousands of Palestinians in the West Bank and Gaza protested against Israeli occupation.
events.short_text: Palestinians launched the first intifada against Israel in 1987.

events.text: In 1993, the Oslo I Accords were signed between Israel and the newly formed Palestinian Authority. The agreement established mutual recognition and laid the foundation for Palestinian self-governance in the West Bank and Gaza.
events.short_text: 1993 Oslo I Accords established Palestinian self-rule and mutual recognition.

events.text: In 1995, the Oslo II Accords expanded the initial agreement by mandating Israel’s withdrawal from six cities and 450 towns in the West Bank, further enabling limited Palestinian self-governance.
events.short_text: 1995 Oslo II Accords mandated Israeli pullback from parts of West Bank.
